coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent MoS2 QDs based on IFE for turn-off determination of FOX-7 in real water samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Shasha
Lv, Jingjing
Pei, Fubin
Lv, Xuchu
Wu, Yi
Hao, Qingli
Zhang, Yuehua
Tong, Zhaoyang
Lei, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A novel method for turn-off sensing 1,1-diamino-2,2-dinitroethylene (FOX-7) in aqueous medium was first proposed based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of FOX-7 on the fluorescence of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs). Water-soluble MoS2 QDs as the fluorophore were prepared by the simple hydrothermal method. The morphology, structure, composition and optical properties of the prepared MoS2 QDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra. The results showed that the MoS2 QDs had good water dispersibility and emitted strong photoluminescence with a particle size of 2 nm. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence signal of MoS2 QDs was quenched in the concentrations range of FOX-7 (0.5–100 μM) and the limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor was 0.19 μM. The method had been applied to analyze the real water samples with good selectivity and stability. Moreover, the quenching mechanism was studied systematically by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and Stern-Volmer equation, which had been proved to be static quenching. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is mainly IFE and electron transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2 QDs
Fluorescence quenching
FOX-7
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing property of the MoS2 QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of MoS2 QDs (a), FOX-7 (b), MoS2 QDs- FOX-7 (c).
HRTEM images of MoS2 QDs at different resolutions. The top and bottom insets in panel B were the SAED pattern and lattice fringe spacing of MoS2 QDs, respectively.
XPS spectra of Mo 3d (A) and S 2p (B).

(A) UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution. The inset was the digital photographs of MoS2 QDs under visible light (right) and 365 nm UV illumination (left). (B) Excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution with excitation wavelength from 295 nm to 345 nm at 10 nm intervals.
(A) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of MoS2 QDs; (B) Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectrum of MoS2 QDs.
pH optimization of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution for FOX-7 detection. Error bars are the standard deviations of three measurements.
Fluorescence spectra of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution after adding 5 μM FOX-7 with the different response time.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution after adding different concentrations of FOX-7 (from a to m: 0, 0.5, 1, 4, 6, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 100 μM, respectively), inset: photographs of MoS2 QDs with 0, 80 and 130 μM FOX-7, respectively (from left to right). (B) The calibration curve of MoS2 QDs for FOX-7 detection. Where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs with and without FOX-7 (λEx = 325 nm).
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of MoS2 QDs in the presence of FOX-7 and other interferences.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of FOX-7, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MoS2 QDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution in the presence of different concentrations of FOX-7.
Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution after adding different concentrations of FOX-7 (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 μM).
Stern-Volmer Plot of MoS2 QDs for FOX-7 (0, 0.5, 1, 4, 6, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 100 μM).
Schematic illustration of MoS2 QDs as the probe for FOX-7 determination.
Comparison between present method and other small molecules based fluorescent nano-sensing method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CS-AuNCs: chondroitin sulfate-stabilized gold nanoclusters; L-Cy-CdS QDs: L-cysteine-coated cadmium sulfide QDs; N-GQDs: nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots; S,N-CQDs: sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots; TNP: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol; EET: electronic energy transfer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative results of detection of water samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent MoS2 QDs based on IFE for turn-off determination of FOX-7 in real water samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel method for turn-off sensing 1,1-diamino-2,2-dinitroethylene (FOX-7) in aqueous medium was first proposed based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of FOX-7 on the fluorescence of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs). Water-soluble MoS2 QDs as the fluorophore were prepared by the simple hydrothermal method. The morphology, structure, composition and optical properties of the prepared MoS2 QDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra. The results showed that the MoS2 QDs had good water dispersibility and emitted strong photoluminescence with a particle size of 2 nm. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence signal of MoS2 QDs was quenched in the concentrations range of FOX-7 (0.5–100 μM) and the limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor was 0.19 μM. The method had been applied to analyze the real water samples with good selectivity and stability. Moreover, the quenching mechanism was studied systematically by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and Stern-Volmer equation, which had been proved to be static quenching. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is mainly IFE and electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: Effective detection of nitro-compounds has attracted considerable attention due to environmental pollution and increasing social security issues [1,2]. Because nitro-compounds are difficult to degrade, the release in the environment can cause harmful hepatotoxicity and blood toxicity to organisms [3,4]. 1,1-diamino-2,2-dinitroethylene (FOX-7) is an explosive of insensitive and high detonation performance, which is considered to be a potential substitute for insensitive explosives used extensively in the military industry [5,6]. Moreover, because of their similarity, it is difficult to distinguish various nitro-compounds explosives, which has limited many analytical methods. So far, the detection of FOX-7 has never been reported. Hence, the development of highly selective and sensitive methods with simple and rapid for FOX-7 detection is urgently required.

Plenty of strategies have been currently developed for detecting nitro compounds, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [7], high performance liquid chromatography [8], mass spectrometry [9] and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [10]. Unfortunately, these methods are limited by time-consuming, expensive instruments and complex samples pretreatment. In contrast, the fluorescence-based sensors exhibited the advantages of operational simplicity, rapid response and high sensitivity [11–13], which have been applied in detection of multiple analytes. The fluorescence intensity (or emission wavelength, probe lifetime) of a fluorescent probe varies with the concentration of a particular analyte, which is the basis of fluorescence sensing. In addition, the low signal-to-noise ratio can be obtained owing to the inherent signal of fluorescence probes without targets [14]. Consequently, the selection of a fluorescent probe directly affects the sensitivity of the sensor. There are several types of fluorescence quenching process, such as inner filter effect (IFE), Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET), among which IFE and FRET are common. As a non-irradiation energy conversion model, IFE does not require surface modification of fluorescent probes or formation of any chemical bonds between a receptor and the fluorescent probe [15]. The fluorescence strategy based on IEF exhibits more considerable flexibility and simplicity than FRET-based method [16].

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials have become hot areas since the researchers discovered graphene in 2004. 2D nanomaterials possess large specific surface area, which are appropriate for many utilizations of surface active [17]. In recent years, graphene-like 2D nanomaterials, such as boron nitride (BN), molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), have widely prospect in application due to their inherent features, including excellent electronic, optical and mechanical properties [18,19]. MoS2 as a representative of 2D transition-metal dichalcogenide with analogous structure to graphene has attracted extensive attention, which has the sandwich structure of S-Mo-S bonded by van der Waals interactions [20]. MoS2 owns new characteristics, since the positions of the adjacent lattice of MoS2 are occupied by different atoms with strong spin-orbit coupling [21]. MoS2 shows strong photoluminescence (PL) intensity due to dielectric confinement effect and quantum size effect, especially MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) and nanosheets with ultrasmall plane size [22,23]. Decreasing the number of layers is beneficial to improving the conductivity and accelerating electron transfer rate [24]. The quantum confinement effect can influence the electronic band structure of materials. Compared with other MoS2 nanostructures, QDs have tunable fluorescence emission, better electron transport facility and higher surface area to volume ratio, which have caused widespread concern in recent years [25,26]. Compared with fluorescence dyes, the QDs possess unique fluorescence performance, such as extremely good photo-stability, wide excitation range, narrow and symmetric emission spectra and so on [27]. Unlike most other QDs with indirect bandgaps, MoS2 QDs exhibit a direct bandgap, which can emit intense fluorescence in the visible range [28]. The semiconductor performance of MoS2 QDs is better, thus MoS2 QDs have greater application prospects in optoelectronic field. MoS2 QDs can be dispersed in aqueous solution without extra oxidation or surfactant treatment and have the advantages of simple preparation and easy functionalization. Due to the excellent features, MoS2 QDs have the potential to be applied to various fields, such as catalysis [29], optoelectronic devices [30], fluorescence sensing [31], and bioimaging [32].

Herein, MoS2 QDs as the fluorescent probe was firstly proposed for turn-off detecting FOX-7 via IFE. For all we know, this is the first report for sensing FOX-7. Small size MoS2 QDs with the required optical properties were prepared through the hydrothermal method, in which sodium molybdate dihydrate and L-cysteine was used as the precursor. Water-soluble MoS2 QDs with outstanding performance as fluorescence probes were used to detect FOX-7 sensitively. The efficiency of fluorescence quenching increased with the increase of FOX-7 concentration, because FOX-7 bound to the surface of MoS2 QDs via producing stable non-fluorescent complex between MoS2 QDs and FOX-7, which facilitated the transfer of electron energy from MoS2 QDs to FOX-7 and then reduced the fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs [33]. This method had been successfully applied to assay FOX-7 in natural water samples and the satisfactory results were obtained. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of MoS2 QDs as the probe for FOX-7 determination.

Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) were bought from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (HCl) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The explosive FOX-7, HMX, Triaminotrinitrobenzene (TATB), 2, 4-dinitroanisole (DNAN), Nitroguanidine (NQ), 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene (TNT), RDX were supplied by the Institute of Propellant Changing Technology, Nanjing University of Science and Technology (Nanjing, Jiangsu). All reagents used were of analytical reagent grade and used without any purification.

All photoluminescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were measured on FL3-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were examined on Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on UV-220 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were obtained by Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed by PHI Quantera II (Ulvac-Phi, Japan).

The MoS2 QDs were prepared through the one-step hydrothermal method with Na2MoO4·2H2O as the precursor according to the previously reported method [34]. First, 0.25 g Na2MoO4·2H2O and 0.5 g L-cysteine were dispersed in 25 mL of water under stirring for 30 min, respectively. Then, the above-mentioned solutions were blended and stirred at 40 °C, adjusting the pH of the solution to 3 with concentrated hydrochloric acid (12 N). The above reactant was poured into 100 mL Teflon autoclave at 200 °C for 42 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the yellow colloidal solution was obtained by collecting supernatant and stored after centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 30 min.

20 μL MoS2 QDs was diluted with 2 mL deionized water, which was placed in a quartz cell and used as the fluorescent probe for FOX-7 detection. The different concentrations of FOX-7 solutions were prepared in water. After the mixtures of MoS2 and various concentrations of FOX-7 solution were incubated for 3 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured from 340 to 600 nm at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The slits for emission and excitation were set at 4 nm.

Fluorescence lifetime measurement: The fluorescence lifetime was recorded by using the LED laser light source at 371 nm as the excitation. The scanning conditions: the excitation and emission wavelength ranges are 295–355 nm and 320–600 nm, respectively, and the voltage of 950 V.

The FT-IR spectra were performed to analyze the surface functional groups. As can be seen from Fig. 1a, the peak at about 3455 cm− 1 belonged to the characteristic band of OH. And the peaks at about 1644 cm− 1 and 1404 cm− 1 were contributing to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively, together with peaks at about 998 cm− 1 due to the CH in-plane bending, demonstrating the remaining L-cysteine molecules located on the surface of the prepared MoS2 QDs. The weak peak at 442 cm− 1 corresponded to MoS vibration. The results were consistent with the reported literature [35].

For FOX-7 (Fig. 1b), the several strong peaks in 3215–3423 cm− 1 correspond to the –NH2 or –NH–. The characteristic peaks in the region of 1332–1620 cm− 1 were ascribed to the –NO2 and CN of FOX-7. The peak at 885 cm− 1 was assigned to –NH2 out-of-plane deformation vibration absorption. Fig. 1c displayed the FT-IR spectrum of MoS2 QDs bound with FOX-7. The characteristic bands at 1717 cm− 1 and 1620 cm− 1 were contributing to the –CO stretching vibration and the –NH– in-plane bending vibration of CO⋯N-H, respectively [36], indicating that new substances were formed between MoS2 QDs and FOX-7. Compared with the free CO peak (~1690 cm− 1), there was a 27 cm− 1 shift in the peak position, which indicates the hydrogen bond in the compound is strong [37].

To investigate the size and morphology of MoS2 QDs, the HRTEM images were recorded. Fig. 2 presented that the QDs exhibited a uniform spherical morphology with the average particle size of about 2 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern showed the MoS2 QDs were crystalline. And it can be seen from the bottom inset that the lattice spacing was 0.23 nm, which corresponding to the (103) plane of MoS2 QDs [38]. The distribution of MoS2 QDs was homogeneous, indicating MoS2 QDs had good dispersibility.

In order to investigate the chemical composition of MoS2 QDs, the binding energies of Mo and S were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). For Mo 3d (Fig. 3A), the two distinct characteristic peaks of Mo were observed at 227.65 eV and 231.94 eV, which belonged to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, revealing the presence of Mo4+ oxidation state in the MoS2 QDs. Fig. 3B gave the S 2p region, the peaks at binding energy of 163.35 eV and 164.63 eV were ascribed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals of S2−, respectively. The results indicated the MoS2 QDs had been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing property of the MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A presented the UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of MoS2 QDs. As can be seen, MoS2 QDs displayed an absorption band near 206 nm (Curve a). Furthermore, the as-prepared aqueous solution of MoS2 QDs had a maximum emission peak (Curve c) at 400 nm upon excitation (Curve b) at 325 nm (Fig. 4A). The inset was the digital photographs of the MoS2 QDs under visible light and 365 nm UV illumination, respectively. The prepared MoS2 QDs under 365 nm UV illumination generated the intense blue luminescence emission, which is attracted to the quantum size effect of MoS2 QDs [39,40]. The excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra were presented in Fig. 4B. With the excitation wavelength increasing from 295 nm to 345 nm, the emission spectra of MoS2 QDs gradually red-shifted. The strongest fluorescence emission intensity was observed under the excitation of 325 nm. Therefore, the optimal excitation wavelength was 325 nm. The results suggested that the MoS2 QDs had a typical excitation-dependent fluorescent characteristic, which was probably ascribed to the quantum confinement effect and the formation of electron-hole pairs at different energy levels formed due to variable particle sizes [41,42].

The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum can display the complete fluorescence spectral characteristics of the probe. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum and the excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectrum of MoS2 QDs were displayed in Fig. 5A and B, respectively. The largest emission area was mainly in the red region with the optimum excitation wavelength of about 325 nm. The result was consistent with Fig. 4B.

The pH is an important influencing factor in the detection system, which can influence the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor. The pH of MoS2 QDs was adjusted with 0.1 M NaOH and HCl. The effect of pH (4–9) on the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution-FOX-7 system was studied. Fig. 6 showed that the emission fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs with and without FOX-7 changed obviously with the pH fluctuations. When the pH was 7, the quenching efficiency was the highest. The quenching intensity decreased sharply whether the pH was less than or greater than 7. When pH was less than 7, it may be that induced intermolecular electrostatic interactions facilitated electron transfer (ET) from MoS2 QDs to FOX-7. When the solution was alkaline, the carboxylic groups were deprotonated to form anionic double layers, inhibiting the generation of fluorescence [43]. So, the optimal pH for the detection system was 7 in this work.

The response time plays an important role in many sensing applications, the designed sensor is expected to respond quickly for FOX-7. But too short response time will make the reaction of MoS2 QDs and FOX-7 incomplete, which results in reducing the quenching efficiency. If response time is long, the superiority of the sensor will lose. Fig. 7 showed the emission spectra of MoS2 QDs after adding 5 μM FOX-7 with different response time. The fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of the incubation time gradually. However, the fluorescence intensity remained almost constant after 3 min. Therefore, 3 min was chosen as the reasonable incubation time.

The sensitivity of the MoS2 QDs-based fluorescence sensor for detecting FOX-7 was measured by recording the change of fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs with various concentrations of FOX-7 solution for 3 min at 400 nm. Fig. 8A displayed that the fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs decreased with the increase of the concentrations of FOX-7 solution from 0.5 to 100 μM and there was no change in the spectral characteristics of MoS2 QDs, denoting no other emitting substances were formed and the sensor for quantitatively detecting FOX-7 was of validity. The inset displayed the photographs of MoS2 QDs with 0, 80 and 130 μM FOX-7, respectively (from left to right), verifying the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased with the increase of FOX-7 concentration, which indicated that FOX-7 bound to the surface of MoS2 QDs via producing stable non-fluorescent complex between MoS2 QDs and FOX-7. The excited-state electron can transfer from MoS2 QDs to FOX-7. Moreover, Fig. 8B showed the fluorescence quenching efficiency was proportional to the FOX-7 concentrations in the ranges of 0.5–25 μM and 25–100 μM, and the linear regression equations were F0-F = 17,606C (μM) + 38,738 (the correlation coefficient r1 = 0.996) and F0-F = 5699C (μM) + 385,409 (r2 = 0.998), respectively, where F0 and F denoted the fluorescence intensity of fluorophore without and with FOX-7. The limit of detection (LOD) for FOX-7 was 0.19 μM (S/N = 3). Compared with the earlier reported fluorescent nano-sensing method for other small molecules (Table 1 ), the fluorescence sensor based on MoS2 QDs for FOX-7 displayed a relatively lower detection limit, shorter response time and wider linear range.

To further evaluate the specificity of the MoS2 QDs for FOX-7, it is investigated that the responses of MoS2 QDs toward various interfering species including structural property analogues (HMX, R-80, TATB, NQ, ONP, TNT and DNAN, 5 μM) and common disturbances (Na+, Ca2+ and glucose, 30 μM) for the determination of FOX-7 under the optimal experimental conditions. Fig. 9 showed the fluorescence responses of MoS2 QDs alone and to an individual interfering condition. There are negligible effects on the fluorescence responses of MoS2 QDs, indicating the fluorescent probe had a good anti-interference ability.

It is a challenge to analyze the target of real samples, because of the presence of unknown interferences. To research practicability of the sensor, the MoS2 QDs were utilized to analyze FOX-7 in real water samples. The tap water, lake water, and river water were obtained from the laboratory, lake of Nanjing University of Science and Technology, and Qinhuai River located in Nanjing, Jiangsu, respectively. The addition and recovery studies were performed by addition known concentration of the FOX-7 standard solution to samples, which were recorded the fluorescence signal of MoS2 QDs at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. As shown in Table 2 , the satisfied recovery rates were obtained, ranging from 99.20%–104.17% with the RSD of less than 5%, which indicated that the proposed sensor had a good acceptation and applicability for FOX-7 in real samples.

To further investigate the quenching mechanism between MoS2 QDs and FOX-7, the optical test of MoS2 QDs was performed to explain the interaction of MoS2 QDs and FOX-7. As can be seen in Fig. 10A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FOX-7 overlapped with both the excitation and emission spectra of MoS2 QDs, suggesting that the quenching mechanism between MoS2 QDs and FOX-7 may be the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Fig. 10B presented the UV–vis absorption spectra of MoS2 QDs aqueous solution in the presence of different concentrations of FOX-7. As can be seen, the absorption intensity increased with the increase of FOX-7 concentration at the wavelength of 205–215 nm and the UV–vis absorption spectra was red-shifted with the increase of FOX-7 concentration, which may be due to form a stable non-fluorescence complex between the MoS2 QDs and FOX-7 [50]. The result was confirmed by the FT-IR spectrum of MoS2 QDs-FOX-7. When adding low concentration of FOX-7, the quenching mechanism of FOX-7 on MoS2 QDs can be attributed to the hydrogen-bond interaction assisted electron transfer [51]. When the adding concentration of FOX-7 exceeded 10 μM, the absorption peak at 350 nm appeared, which corresponded to that of FOX-7.

In general, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching includes static and dynamic quenching. The fluorescence lifetime testing is the most powerful method to distinguish between static and dynamic quenching. The fluorescence lifetime is proportional to the concentration of quencher for dynamic quenching. However, the fluorescence decay lifetime of the material remains unchanged with the increase of quencher concentration for static quenching [52]. To further identify the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, lifetime measurement was carried out. The fluorescence lifetime attenuation curves of MoS2 QDs before and after FOX-7 addition were presented in Fig. 11 . The fitting of fluorescence lifetime is almost the same before and after the addition of FOX-7, conforming there was no fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between MoS2 QDs and FOX-7 [53,54]. Because τ0/τ = 1, it can be decided that the system was mainly static quenching instead of dynamic quenching. It was speculated that there may be an IFE. In the detection system, MoS2 QDs and FOX-7 served as fluorescence reporter and absorber, respectively.
The fluorescence quenching procedure also can be represented by Stern-Volmer function accurately [55].

For static quenching,
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K S
Q

For dynamic quenching,
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
K D
Q
(3)
F 0
/ F =
τ 0
/ τ

For another case, the coexistence of static and dynamic quenching:
(4)
F 0
F
=
1 +
K D
Q
1 +
K S
Q
= 1 +
K D
+
K S
Q
+
K D
K S
Q
2

KS, kq and KD are the static quenching constant, molecular quenching constant and the dynamic quenching constant, respectively. [Q] is the quencher concentration. τ0 and τ are the lifetimes of fluorophore without and with quencher.

In this work, the quenching procedure conformed to Eq. (1) perfectly with the concentration range of 0–100 μM (Fig. 12 ), which showed that the static quenching mechanism occurred. The result was consistent with that of the lifetime measurement. The KS was calculated to be 24.1 × 103 L/mol, which representing the affinity of quencher and fluorophore.

In summary, a novel photoluminescence sensor was proposed for detecting FOX-7 for the first time. And the sensing mechanism based on the IFE of FOX-7 on the fluorescence of water-soluble MoS2 QDs was systematically studied. FOX-7 and MoS2 QDs were used as the IFE absorber and fluorophore, respectively. The morphology and composition of water-soluble MoS2 QDs with the particle size of about 2 nm were verified by TEM, XPS and FT-IR. Under optimal experimental conditions, the sensor can effectively detect FOX-7 ranging from 0.5 μM to 100 μM with LOD of 0.19 μM and the Stern-Volmer quenching constant of 2.41 × 104 L/mol was obtained. The sensor exhibited high sensitivity, short response and detection time. The sensing strategy was applied to analyze FOX-7 in water samples with satisfactory recovery rates. This work proposes a novel and effective method for detecting FOX-7 and expands the application of MoS2 QDs in the field of sensing.

Shasha Feng: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jingjing Lv: Validation, Methodology. Fubin Pei: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xuchu Lv: Data curation, Software. Yi Wu: Investigation. Qingli Hao: Resources, Funding acquisition. Yuehua Zhang: Visualization. Zhaoyang Tong: Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Wu Lei: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51572127, 21576138, 51872140), Program for NCET-12-0629, Ph.D. Program Foundation of Ministry of Education of China (No. 20133219110018), Six Major Talent Summit (XNY-011), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20160828), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (1501016B) and PAPD of Jiangsu Province, China.
We also thank Dr. Wanying Tang at Analysis and Test Center Nanjing University of Science and Technology for the FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra data collection.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A single molecular fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of nitroaromatic explosives: a new strategy for the mask-free discrimination of TNT and TNP within same sample
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhang, S.S. Chen, R. Shi, J.W. Ji, D.Q. Wang, S.H. Jin, T.Y. Han, C.X. Zhou, Q.H. Shu, A single molecular fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of nitroaromatic explosives: A new strategy for the mask-free discrimination of TNT and TNP within same sample, Talanta 166 (2017) 228-233.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodamine-isonicotinic hydrazide analogue: a selective fluorescent chemosensor for the nanomolar detection of picric acid in aqueous media
ChemistrySelect
